summary: Joel Spolsky continues a discussion about elevator usability, sharing two anecdotes that illustrate how deeply ingrained habits affect user interaction with systems. He describes how users of modern floor-selecting elevators sometimes stand passively in their home building's traditional elevators, expecting the system to already know their destination. He also shares a classic story about solving perceived elevator slowness by installing mirrors rather than speeding up the elevators.
key_points: Users carry mental models from one system into another, leading to errors when interfaces differ between similar products
Elevators are an ideal usability case study because everyone uses them yet their design is full of subtle pitfalls
Sometimes the best engineering solution addresses the user's perception of a problem rather than the technical problem itself
money_quotes: Elevators, actually, are a great usability subject, because so many people are familiar with them, and they seem like they should be so simple, but pitfalls are everywhere.

People who work in buildings with the new elevators but live in buildings with traditional elevators report that when they get home at night, they sometimes get into the elevator and then just stand there, expecting the elevator to know their floor already.
They had the hotel install mirrors in the hallway where the elevators were. By the time the visitors were done preening, the elevator had arrived. Human engineering at its finest.
key_insight: The best usability solutions often address human perception and habit rather than optimizing the underlying system itself.
